coredata.dc:description: Chronic exposure to oxidants such as benzoyl peroxide (BPO) and hypochlorite (ClO−) poses significant health risks, driving the urgent need for sensitive detection methods. We developed a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence probe based on europium metal-organic frameworks encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@Eu-MOFs). This probe enables rapid detection within 1 min for BPO and 3 min for ClO−, demonstrating broad linear ranges (0.1–6.0 μM) with ultralow detection limits (6.80 nM for BPO, 5.78 nM for ClO−). N-CDs@Eu-MOFs successfully quantified both analytes in five real food matrices, showing recovery rates of 91.94–108.33 % for BPO and 91.25–107.22 % for ClO−, confirming its reliability in real-world applications. Additionally, portable N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-embedded gel beads were fabricated that facilitate smartphone-based BPO quantification through RGB analysis, achieving recovery rates of 91.41–105.44 % in food samples. This instrument-free approach combines laboratory-grade accuracy with on-site practicality, significantly advancing food safety monitoring.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of benzoyl peroxide and hypochlorite in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chronic exposure to oxidants such as benzoyl peroxide (BPO) and hypochlorite (ClO−) poses significant health risks, driving the urgent need for sensitive detection methods. We developed a dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence probe based on europium metal-organic frameworks encapsulated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@Eu-MOFs). This probe enables rapid detection within 1 min for BPO and 3 min for ClO−, demonstrating broad linear ranges (0.1–6.0 μM) with ultralow detection limits (6.80 nM for BPO, 5.78 nM for ClO−). N-CDs@Eu-MOFs successfully quantified both analytes in five real food matrices, showing recovery rates of 91.94–108.33 % for BPO and 91.25–107.22 % for ClO−, confirming its reliability in real-world applications. Additionally, portable N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-embedded gel beads were fabricated that facilitate smartphone-based BPO quantification through RGB analysis, achieving recovery rates of 91.41–105.44 % in food samples. This instrument-free approach combines laboratory-grade accuracy with on-site practicality, significantly advancing food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Benzoyl peroxide (BPO) and hypochlorite (ClO−) are widely utilized oxidizing agents in food and water treatment, respectively. BPO serves as an effective preservative by inhibiting lipid oxidation and enzymatic browning (Dong et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2019), yet its application raises safety concerns. Chronic exposure may degrade nutritional quality and generate genotoxic byproducts (e.g., biphenyl, benzyl benzoate), causing DNA damage and oxidative stress. Consequently, the World Health Organization (WHO) classifies BPO as a Group 3 carcinogen (Lin et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017). Similarly, ClO− a widely utilized disinfectant in postharvest preservation of agricultural products and drinking water treatment (Yu et al., 2022), has raised significant public health concerns due to its potential toxicological effects. Chronic exposure to ClO− may be associated with multiple systemic pathologies, including cardiovascular dysfunction, respiratory disorders, hepatotoxicity, and carcinogenic potential. As a result, ClO− is also listed as a Group 3 carcinogen by the WHO (Suna, Erdemir, Gunduz, Ozturk, & Karakus, 2023; Yu et al., 2022). Therefore, the potential health risks associated with BPO and ClO− highlight the urgent need for reliable detection methods to ensure the safety of food.

Fluorescence detection has emerged as a superior alternative to conventional techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), as well as emerging techniques like surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) (Abe-Onishi, Yomota, Sugimoto, Kubota, & Tanamoto, 2004; Traore, Lazaro, & Gay, 1997; Wang, Zhang, Li, & Tian, 2016; Zhao et al., 2023), offering distinct advantages in rapidity, sensitivity, selectivity, cost-effectiveness, and operational simplicity (Peng et al., 2021; Xia et al., 2022). Currently, fluorescent probes are primarily classified into two categories: organic small molecules (Wang, Liu, Niu, & Liu, 2023; Wanderi & Cui, 2022) and fluorescent nanomaterials (Chang et al., 2024; Xia et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2023). While organic probes demonstrate utility, their limitations in synthetic complexity, biological toxicity, and environmental instability (Wang et al., 2021) have shifted research focus towards nanomaterial-based systems.

Recent advances highlight carbon dots (CDs) and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) as highly promising fluorescent nanomaterials. CDs (<10 nm) exhibit exceptional optical tunability (Xu, Lin & Chang, 2020), biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2023), and scalable synthesis (Zu et al., 2017), with expanding applications in bioimaging, antimicrobial systems, and anti-counterfeiting technologies (Chan et al., 2021; Dong, Liang, Meziani, Sun, & Yang, 2020; Guo et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2024). MOFs complement these properties through ultrahigh surface areas, structural modularity, and enhanced luminescence when functionalized with rare-earth elements, enabling versatile applications in catalysis, gas storage, and sensing (Solis et al., 2020; Tan et al., 2024; Xia et al., 2024). Strategically integrating CDs and MOFs yields a hybrid material where CDs are encapsulated within an MOF matrix. This configuration enhances fluorescence intensity and creates a dual-emission system. Within this composite, the MOF acts as a carrier for both the CDs and rare-earth metals, synergistically amplifying the fluorescent signal for improved sensing performance (Wang, Jin, Yan, Yang & Liu, 2023).

In this study, blue-green fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized via solvothermal treatment of citric acid, urea, and tryptophan. Following this, a novel composite material, europium-based metal-organic frameworks encapsulating nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs@Eu-MOFs), was constructed via room-temperature assembly of the N-CDs with 1,3,5-phenyltrimethoxyl acid, EuCl₃, and sodium acetate, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . This system leverages dual-emission from two distinct materials to provide an internal standard effect, significantly minimizing experimental errors in BPO and ClO− detection. The composite exhibited high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and strong anti-interference properties towards both analytes. Importantly, visual detection of BPO was achieved by monitoring the RGB value of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-loaded sodium alginate gel beads under UV light, thereby simplifying the detection process.

Citric acid (99.5 %), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5 %), benzoic acid (99.5 %), benzoyl peroxide (99.5 %), 1,3,5-phenyltrimethoic acid (98 %), EuCl3·6H2O (99.99 %), and sodium acetate (NaAc·3H2O, 99 %) were obtained from Aladdin reagent (China). Urea (98 %), thiourea (99 %), and sodium hypochlorite (NaClO, 60 %) were obtained from Macklin reagent (China). The following compounds were sourced from Blue Gene Biotech (Shanghai, China): cysteine (Cys, 99 %), phenylalanine (Phe, 99 %), glycine (Gly, 99 %), lysine (Lys, 99 %), glutamic acid (Glu, 99 %), leucine (Leu, 99 %), tryptophan (Trp, 99 %), isoleucine (Ile, 99.5 %), aspartic acid (Asp, 99 %), valine (Val, 99 %), glutathione (99 %), vitamin B3 (VB3, 99 %), glucose (99 %), sucrose (96 %), lecithin (99 %), and allantoin (98 %). Potassium iodide (KI, 99 %) was purchased from Xilong Chemical Reagent (China). The following salts were obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent (Tianjin, China): sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99 %), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 97 %), sodium fluoride (NaF, 98 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.5 %), lithium chloride (LiCl, 97 %), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 96 %), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99 %), copper sulfate (CuSO4, 99 %), manganese chloride (MnCl2, 99 %), barium chloride (BaCl2, 99.5 %), cadmium chloride (CdCl2, 99.99 %), nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2, 99 %), zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2, 99.99 %), and chromium chloride (CrCl3, 99 %). Glycerol (96 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), benzaldehyde (98.5 %), and ethanol (99.7 %) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group.

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed using an FEI-Tecnai G2 20. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) was performed using a Bruker/Dimension Icon. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) results were sourced from a HITACHI SU8010. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out on a Bruker-D8 Advanced using CuKα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained from a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. Fourier Transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) results were recorded using a Thermofisher icolet iS10. Zeta potential was carried out using a Nano ZS + MPT-2. Fluorometric spectra were recorded on a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 using xenon lamps. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra (UV–Vis) data were collected on an INESA N4S spectrophotometer.

Preparation of N-CDs The synthesis of N-CDs was accomplished via a solvothermal approach. A homogeneous mixture of citric acid (3.844 g, 20 mmol), urea (7.208 g, 120 mmol), tryptophan (2.042 g, 10 mmol), and DMF (40 mL) was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The reaction was heated at 180 °C for 12 h and then allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature. The resultant dark brown-red solution was centrifuged at 10,700 g for 10 min to remove large particle precipitates. To the supernatant, anhydrous ethanol (80 mL) was slowly added under stirring, inducing precipitation. The precipitate was collected by centrifugation (10,700 g, 10 min), washed thrice with ethanol (40 mL each), and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h，yielding 759.2 mg of black powder (N-CDs).

Preparation of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs A solution of EuCl3·6H2O (410 mg, 1.25 mmol) and NaAc·3H2O (244.9 mg, 1.8 mmol) in water (40 mL) was designated as Liquid A. Liquid B was prepared by dissolving 1,3,5-phthalic acid (210 mg, 1 mmol) dissolved in ethanol (45 mL). Liquid C was prepared by dissolving N-CDs (60 mg) in DMF (5 mL). To synthesize N-CDs@Eu-MOFs, Liquid C was added to Liquid A under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, Liquid B was introduced into the mixture and stirring was continued at room temperature for 1 h. The resulting suspension was centrifuged, and the precipitate was collected and washed sequentially with DMF, water, and ethanol (three times each), followed by centrifugation after each wash. The isolated solid was dried under vacuum for 12 h, yielding 352.3 mg of deep purple powder (N-CDs@Eu-MOFs). For subsequent experiments, the powder was then dispersed in water to a final concentration of 0.25 mg/mL.

Preparation of Eu-MOFs The synthesis of Eu-MOFs followed an identical protocol to that of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs, with the omission of N-CDs in precursor solution C. This modification yielded a final white powder (Eu-MOFs).

To the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension (0.25 mg/mL, 2.00 mL), add varying volumes (0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, and 6.00 μL) of a standard BPO solution (2.00 mM in DMF). This results in final BPO concentrations of 0.10, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, and 6.00 μM, respectively. After thorough mixing, the fluorescence emission spectrum of each mixture was acquired.

To the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension (0.25 mg/mL, 2 mL), add 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, and 6 μL of a standard ClO− solution (2 mM in H2O). This results in final ClO− concentrations of 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 5, and 6 μM, respectively. Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of each mixture were recorded.

The fluorescence response of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs to potential substances contained in several real samples were investigated. To this end, 12 μL of each interferent (10 mM) was mixed with 2 mL of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs solution (0.25 mg/mL), and the resulting mixture was stirred thoroughly. With each analyte's concentration being 60 μM in the end, the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was subsequently recorded. For the anti-interference test, 6 μL of BPO or ClO− (2 mM) was introduced into the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs mixture already containing one of the potential interferents. The resulted final concentrations of BPO (or ClO−) and each potential disruptor were 6 μM and 60 μM, respectively. After mixing, the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was acquired.
BPO or ClO− (6 μM) was introduced into 2 mL suspensions of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (0.25 mg/mL), and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded every 2 min over a 15-min period.

All real samples (wheat flour, starch, bread, biscuits, tap water, milk, eggs, cucumbers, and apples) were sourced from a local supermarket in Wuhan, China. Solid powdery samples (wheat flour, starch, bread, biscuits) underwent ethanol extraction (5 g sample in 20 mL ethanol, 30-min sonication) followed by centrifugation (6880 g, 5 min). The supernatant was membrane-filtered (0.45 μm) to obtain extracts. Tap water (sourced from Hubei University of Technology) was directly filtered through 0.45 μm membranes. For milk, a threefold volume of ethanol was added prior to vortex mixing, centrifugation (6880 g, 5 min), and 0.45 μm filtration; the resultant extract was diluted fivefold with deionized water. Fresh produce (eggs, cucumbers, apples) was immersed in deionized water (1:5, w/v) for 20 min, with the soakate subsequently filtered (0.45 μm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis BPO and ClO− in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Prepare stock standard solutions of BPO (in DMF) and ClO− (in H2O) at concentrations of 6, 16, and 36 mM. Spike 20 μL of the appropriate BPO or ClO− stock solution into each 2 mL of aliquots of each pretreated sample solution to achieve spiked final BPO or ClO− concentrations of 60, 160, and 360 μM. Then, transfer 20 μL of these treatment solutions into 2 mL of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs suspension (0.25 mg/mL in water), resulting in final BPO or ClO− concentrations of 0.6, 1.6, and 3.6 μM. Perform fluorescence measurements in triplicate for each final mixture. Record the emission spectra using an excitation wavelength of 295 nm and slit widths of 4 nm for both excitation and emission monochromators.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of BPO content in recovery
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of BPO was calculated based on the linear relationship between the intensity of the UV absorbance peak at 233 nm (Gaddipati, Volpe, & Anthony, 1983; Supharoek, Ponhong, & Grudpan, 2017) and its concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection BPO using sodium alginate gel beads
Preparation of sodium alginate gel beads
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium alginate (2 g) was dissolved in water (40 mL) under vigorous stirring at 75 °C until complete dissolution was achieved. Concurrently, a dispersion of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (30 mg) in water (10 mL) was prepared. After cooling the sodium alginate solution to room temperature, it was thoroughly mixed with the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs dispersion. The combined mixture was introduced dropwise via a 5 mL syringe into a saturated CaCl2 solution. Following bead solidification, the gel beads were rinsed with distilled water to remove residual CaCl2 and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

The gel beads were incubated in DMF solutions containing BPO concentrations (1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 12, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, and 50 μM) for 4 h. Afterward, the beads were removed, rinsed briefly with water, and photographed under 365 nm UV light. The images were analyzed using smartphone-based RGB color recognition software to determine green-to-red (G/R) intensity ratios. The linear relationship between BPO concentration and G/R values was then quantified.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable detection BPO in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The five ethanolic-pretreated real samples (wheat flour, starch, biscuits, bread, and noodles) were vacuum-dried at 40 °C to eliminate the ethanol solvent. The resulting residues were then reconstituted in DMF to prepare sample-specific pretreatment solutions. For each solution, 1.2, 3.2, and 6.4 μL of BPO standard solution (10 mM in DMF) were spiked into 2 mL of the pretreatment DMF solution for real samples, respectively, yielding final BPO concentrations of 6, 16, and 32 μM, respectively. The N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-loaded gel beads were then immersed in these spiked real samples solutions containing varying concentrations of BPO. The G/B values were quantified and analyzed.

The TEM imaging of N-CDs (Fig. 1a) exhibits a spherical structure and good dispersibility. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) imaging (inset in Fig. 1a) shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, attributable to the (102) plane of graphite (Feng, Shi, Chang, Dong, & Shuang, 2024). Statistical analysis indicates that the average diameter of N-CDs is 9.82 nm (Fig. 1b). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) results confirm that N-CDs have excellent dispersibility, with an estimated height range of 1.0 to 2.4 nm (Fig. 1d), suggestive of N-CDs composed of 3 to 8 stacked graphene-like layers, consistent with TEM observations. The powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) pattern of N-CDs (Fig. 1c) exhibits a broad diffraction peak centered at 27.1°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (Stachowska et al., 2021).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis characterized the elemental composition, functional groups, and bonding properties of N-CDs. Deconvoluted C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s spectra are presented in Fig. 1g-i. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1g) displays peaks at 284.59 eV (C–C/C=C), 285.47 eV (CN), 287.56 eV (CO), and 290.32 eV (O–C=O) (Bandi et al., 2016; Huang et al., 2019; Stachowska et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2021). The N 1 s spectrum is splits into two peaks at 399.89 and 401.94 eV, attributed to CN and NH, respectively (Tang et al., 2022). The O 1 s spectrum also splits into two peaks at 531.09 and 532.35 eV, corresponding to CO and CO (Wang, Wu, Xing & Wang, 2022).

The PXRD profiles of Eu-MOFs and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1c) indicate that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs retain crystallinity of Eu-MOFs, with diffraction peaks matching the simulated pattern (CCDC: 290771). The small size of N-CDs allows for their embedding within the Eu-MOFs without disturbing the crystal structure (Yi, Zhang, & Zhang, 2020). SEM images (Fig. 1e, f) comparing Eu-MOFs and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs show that the size of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs is significantly larger than that of pure Eu-MOFs. This size increase is likely attributable to interaction between the abundant –NH2 groups on the surface of N-CDs and the –COOH groups of the Eu-MOFs precursor, influencing the growth of Eu-MOFs (Wang et al., 2024). Additionally, N-CDs@Eu-MOFs display pronounced aggregation, likely due to the in-situ growth of Eu-MOFs in the presence of N-CDs (Wang et al., 2020).

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Fig. 1j) shows no significant differences between the spectra of Eu-MOFs and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs. According to the proportion of Eu3+ in Eu-MOFs and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (Table S1), the amount of N-CDs doped is calculated to be about 25 %, indicating that this result is attributed to the low doping amount of N-CDs.

XPS analysis of N-CDs, Eu-MOFs, and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1k-o and Table S1) confirmed successful composite formation. The survey spectra (Fig. 1k) show that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs contain Eu, C, and O (characteristic of Eu-MOFs), along with a distinct N 1 s signal originating from N-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1l, the N 1 s spectrum of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs can be decomposed into two characteristic peaks attributed to NH and CN bonds, whereas the N 1 s characteristic peak is almost absent in Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1m). Additionally, the Eu 3d spectra of both Eu-MOFs and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1n, o) exhibit nearly identical characteristic peaks, which can be assigned to the Eu3+ 3d3/2 and Eu3+ 3d5/2 transitions. This further indicates the successful construction of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (Alammar et al., 2018; Yi et al., 2020).

As shown in Fig. 2a, the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibit a fluorescence emission peak at 480 nm, which originates from the N-CDs, confirming the successful encapsulation of N-CDs within the framework. Additionally, the UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 2b) reveals characteristic absorption peaks of N-CDs at 450 nm and 550 nm in the composite material, providing further evidence for the successful encapsulation of N-CDs in the Eu-MOFs.

The stability of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs was systematically evaluated (Fig. 2c-f). For temporal stability assessment, an aqueous suspension (12.5 mg in 50 mL H2O) of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs was maintained at ambient temperature. Fluorescence spectra were documented hourly over 8 h revealed minimal intensity variation at 480 nm (N-CDs) and 617 nm (Eu3+) (Fig. 2c), indicating that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs possess a certain time stability. Under continuous UV irradiation (313 nm, 8 W) for 90 min, the Eu3+ emission at 617 nm remained stable, while the N-CD peak at 480 nm exhibited ∼40 % quenching (Fig. 2d), indicating partial photobleaching of the N-CDs component.

pH stability tests (Fig. 2e) demonstrated high stability of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs within the pH range of 4 to 9, as evidenced by consistent the fluorescence intensity ratios at 617 and 480 nm at different pH values. Additionally, a 2.0 mL suspension of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs (0.25 mg/mL) was prepared, and 20 μL of NaCl solutions with varying concentrations were added. The resulting fluorescence spectra indicated that as the NaCl concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity ratios at 617 and 480 nm only slightly increased within a certain range, suggesting that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs maintain relative stability when NaCl is present (Fig. 2f).

Optimal detection conditions for N-CDs@Eu-MOFs were established by optimizing concentration and excitation wavelength. When the concentration of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs varied between 0.025 and 1 mg/mL (Fig. S1a), the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 480 nm exhibited a concentration-dependent increase followed by self-quenching at higher concentrations, reaching its peak at 0.25 mg/mL. Similarly, the Eu3+ fluorescence intensity also peaked at 0.25 mg/mL. When the excitation wavelength was varied between 265 and 315 nm (Fig. S1b), the fluorescence intensity peaks for both N-CDs and Eu3+ were highest at an excitation wavelength of 295 nm. Therefore, a concentration of 0.25 mg/mL and an excitation wavelength of 295 nm were selected for the subsequent experiments.

To assess the sensitivity of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs to BPO and ClO−, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs at various standard concentrations of BPO and ClO− (ranging from 0 to 6 μM) were measured. As shown in Fig. 3a and c, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs at 480 nm gradually decreases with the increasing concentration of BPO or ClO−. When detecting BPO or ClO−, the fluorescence intensity ratio of I 617/I 480 exhibited two distinct linear relationships within the concentration ranges of 0.1–2.5 μM and 2.5–6 μM (Fig. 3b and d). For BPO, the linear relationship equations were: I 617/I 480 = 0.1352C BPO + 0.6548 (0.1–2.5 μM, R2 = 0.9962) and I 617/I 480 = 0.0771C BPO + 0.8019 (2.5–6 μM, R2 = 0.9965) (Fig. 3b). For ClO−, the linear equations were: I 617/I 480 = 0.1591C ClO − + 0.6384 (0.1–2.5 μM, R2 = 0.9982) and I 617/I 480 = 0.0329C ClO- + 0.9470 (2.5–6 μM, R2 = 0.9965) (Fig. 3d). These results demonstrate clear linear correlations between the fluorescence intensity ratio and the concentrations of BPO and ClO−.

The limits of detection (LOD) were calculated to be 6.80 nM for BPO and 5.78 nM for ClO−, based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3. Meanwhile, based on a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 10, the limits of quantification (LOQ) for BPO and ClO− were calculated to be 22.71 and 19.30 nM, respectively. Although the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs probe does not show an advantage in detection limits compared to N-CDs alone (for BPO, LOD = 6.77 nM; for ClO−, LOD = 7.48 nM, Fig. S2a-d), it offers the benefit of an internal standard function, which significantly reduces errors during the detection process. However, when compared with other reported probes, the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs probe demonstrates significant advantages in sensitivity for detecting both BPO and ClO− (Table S2 and S3). These results highlight the ratiometric response, multifunctionality, and high sensitivity of the synthesized N-CDs@Eu-MOFs, establishing it as an effective tool for the trace detection of BPO and ClO−.

The fluorescence signal of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs to multiple substances was explored by adding 43 potential components found in real samples to N-CDs@Eu-MOFs solutions, and recording the fluorescence spectra of each mixture. As indicated in Fig. 4a, S3a, and 4b, S3b, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs remained largely unchanged when 41 substances other than BPO and ClO− were added. BPO and ClO− were the only substances that effectively quenched the fluorescence intensity at 480 nm. These results demonstrate that the fluorescence of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibits a high degree of selectivity towards BPO and ClO−.

Since real samples often contain various substances and oxidants (such as BPO or ClO−), it is essential to investigate the effect of potential interfering substances. To this end, 6 μL of 2 mM BPO or ClO− was incorporated into the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs solution containing interferents (60 μM), and the fluorescence emission spectra of each mixture were documented. As depicted in Fig. 4c, S3c, 4d, and S3d, the fluorescence of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs can be effectively quenched at 480 nm in the presence of interfering substances upon the addition of BPO or ClO−. The findings reveal that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs display outstanding anti-interference performance for detecting BPO and ClO−, highlighting its potential for practical applications in actual samples.

Reaction time is another critical parameter for evaluating sensor performance. Fig. S4a and S4b show the response speed of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs to BPO and ClO−. After adding 6 μM BPO to the N-CDs@Eu-MOF system, the absorbance at 480 nm decreased significantly within 1 min. Similarly, upon adding 6 μM ClO−, N-CDs@Eu-MOFs also exhibited a notable response within 3 min. These results clearly demonstrate that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibit rapid response characteristics to both BPO and ClO−, with shorter response times.

The UV–Vis spectroscopy analysis (Fig. 5a and b) revealed a substantial reduction in the absorption peaks of N-CDs at 451 nm and 548 nm following their interaction with BPO or ClO−. Similarly, upon adding BPO or ClO− to the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs system, the absorption peaks at 451 nm and 548 nm also exhibited a notable reduction. This suggests that the N-CDs in both the N-CDs system and N-CDs@Eu-MOFs system underwent oxidation during their reaction with BPO and ClO−. FT-IR spectroscopy analysis (Fig. 5c) provided additional evidence for the oxidation of N-CDs upon the introduction of BPO or ClO−. Specifically, the oxidation of C–OH functional groups on the carbon dots' surface to –CHO caused a decrease in the absorption peak at 3173 cm−1 (indicative of N–H/O–H) and mad the stretching vibration of –CHO at 2795 cm−1 became more noticeable. Additionally, absorption peaks in the range of 1165–1690 cm−1 were attributed to various functional groups, such as –COOH, CO, CN, and CC. The peak at 1577 cm−1 shifted to 1549 cm−1, providing further evidence for the increase in –COOH and CO groups due to oxidation. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 1370 cm−1 shifted to 1346 cm−1 and intensified, indicating the conversion of –NH2 to –NO under the oxidative influence of BPO or ClO−. Zeta potential measurements (Fig. 5d) showed a slight decrease in the negative potential of N-CDs after the addition of BPO and ClO−, with values dropping from −28.9 mV to −26.9 mV and − 26.3 mV, respectively. This indicates that the –OH groups on the surface of N-CDs were consumed during the oxidation reaction, which led to a reduction in negatively charged groups (such as hydroxyl groups), and consequently, a decrease in the negative potential (Bu et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2019). This result is consistent with the findings from UV–Vis and FT-IR analyses, further supporting the oxidation process of N-CDs. A schematic diagram illustrating the oxidation reaction of N-CDs is showed in Fig. 5e.

To evaluate the feasibility of N-CDs@Eu-MOFs in practical applications, they were used to detect real samples (5 types each) with standard concentrations of BPO or ClO−. The final concentrations of BPO and ClO− were set at 0.6, 1.6, and 3.6 μM, respectively, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded. As presented in Table 1 and Table 2 , the recovery rate for BPO ranged from 91.94 % to 108.33 %, while the recovery rate for ClO− was between 91.25 % and 107.22 %, with acceptable relative standard deviation (RSD) values (n = 3). The recovery results of BPO were validated using the linear relationship between the characteristic UV peak intensity and concentration (as shown in Fig. S5 and Table 1). The results demonstrated that the recovery was satisfactory, and that N-CDs@Eu-MOFs can be efficiently used for quantifying BPO and ClO− in practical samples. Additionally, although several samples obtained through regular channels did not contain detectable levels of BPO or ClO−, this does not mean that the potential risks can be ignored. Therefore, we believe that the detection of BPO and ClO− in real samples holds a certain degree of practical significance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of sodium alginate gel beads for BPO
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig.6a, the preparation process of the sodium alginate gel beads are illustrated. Freshly prepared sodium alginate gel beads loaded with N-CDs@Eu-MOFs emit a faint blue-green light under ultraviolet radiation. After soaking in DMF for 4 h, the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-loaded sodium alginate gel beads emit an attractive pink fluorescence (as shown in the photo taken under ultraviolet light after step 2).

The RGB mode, which uses red (R), green (G), and blue (B) to accurately represent colors, minimizes subjective color perception errors (Chen et al., 2020). To detect BPO, we utilized a smartphone with a free RGB color value app. BPO detection was achieved by analyzing the G/R values of the fluorescence image.

As shown in Fig. 6b, the prepared gel beads were added to BPO solutions of varying concentrations, without any additional background correction. Under 365 nm ultraviolet light, the fluorescence image of the beads was captured with a mobile phone camera. According to the CIE chromaticity diagram (Fig. S6), the fluorescent color of the gel beads progressively shifted from pink to blue-green upon the addition of BPO. This obvious color shift provides a clear and intuitive visual signal for BPO detection.

Further analysis revealed that, as shown in Fig. 6c, the G/B value of the gel beads' fluorescence image exhibited a strong linear correlation with BPO concentration within the range of 0–50 μM (R2 = 0.9942). The LOD for the visual detection of BPO was calculated to be 0.33 μM. When testing real samples, the recovery rate for BPO ranged from 91.41 % to 105.44 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of below 5 % (see Table 3 for detailed data). Therefore, the N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-loaded sodium alginate gel beads offer an intuitive and convenient method for the real-time detection of BPO, which holds significant potential for practical applications.

N-CDs@Eu-MOFs were synthesized at room temperature by stirring 1,3,5-phthalic acid, EuCl3·6H2O, and pre-synthesized N-CDs. As a ratiometric fluorescence probe, N-CDs@Eu-MOFs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for detecting BPO and ClO−, with LODs of 6.80 and 5.78 nM, respectively. It successfully quantified the concentration of BPO and ClO− in food-related real samples, providing a simple and reliable method for detecting these substances in real samples. Additionally, N-CDs@Eu-MOFs-loaded sodium alginate gel beads enabled instrument-free BPO detection via fluorescence imaging under 365 nm UV light. Using smartphone RGB analysis app, the G/R intensity ratio was calculated, offering an intuitive, portable approach for practical applications.
